findings.summary: StageResult defined independently in both checkpoint.py and pipeline_reporter.py with overlapping but different fields
evidence: checkpoint.py:22-32 defines StageResult with fields: name, exit_code, duration, timestamp, completed, status
pipeline_reporter.py:23-42 defines a separate StageResult with fields: name, exit_code, duration, status, output_files, errors, warnings
pipeline.py also defines PipelineStageResult — three coexisting stage result types with overlapping semantics
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into a single StageResult in infrastructure/core/pipeline.py or a shared models module; have the reporting layer extend or wrap it rather than redefine it.

findings.summary: pipeline_reporter.py imports MultiProjectResult under TYPE_CHECKING but accesses live attributes at runtime, making the dependency real but invisible to static analysis
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py:17-18: 'if TYPE_CHECKING: from infrastructure.core.multi_project import MultiProjectResult'
pipeline_reporter.py:98-99: accesses result.successful_projects, result.failed_projects, result.total_duration at runtime — live attribute reads on the guarded type
findings.suggestion: Either import MultiProjectResult unconditionally (it is a genuine runtime dependency) or define a narrower Protocol at the reporting boundary capturing only the attributes needed.

findings.summary: Two parallel error systems (exceptions.py class hierarchy and errors.py string-constant system) coexist without a clear documented boundary
evidence: exceptions.py provides a full exception class hierarchy (TemplateError, ValidationError, etc.) with 48 importers
errors.py provides a separate InfraError dataclass system with string-formatted error constants — only 4 importers
pipeline.py imports from both systems simultaneously with no documented boundary rule
findings.suggestion: Document the boundary explicitly: errors.py for formatted log-message constants, exceptions.py for raiseable types. Remove overlap where InfraError constants duplicate exception class intent.

findings.summary: pipeline_reporter.py performs a redundant local import of json inside generate_test_report(), inconsistent with module-level imports everywhere else
evidence: Line 9: import json (module-level)
Line ~677: import json appears inside generate_test_report() body — the only in-function import of a stdlib module across all infrastructure modules
findings.suggestion: Remove the redundant in-function import; the module-level import is sufficient.

findings.summary: Large population of infrastructure modules missing get_logger call that 87/136 sibling modules use
evidence: Blind packet sibling_behavior: 87/136 infrastructure modules use get_logger; 49+ are flagged as outliers missing it
Modules with real logic (generator.py, pipeline_runner.py, package.py, platforms.py) lack loggers while their siblings all have them
findings.suggestion: Add 'from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger' and 'logger = get_logger(__name__)' to all logic-bearing modules missing it. Pure data/constants modules are acceptable exceptions.

findings.summary: Several pipeline stage scripts omit log_header/log_success that 14/18 and 12/18 sibling scripts use respectively
evidence: Blind packet scripts/ shared_patterns: log_header in 14/18, log_success in 12/18
scripts/02_run_analysis.py missing get_logger, log_header, and log_success entirely
scripts/06_llm_review.py and show_project_info.py missing log_success at successful completion
findings.suggestion: Align outlier scripts to the established pattern: log_header at entry and log_success at successful completion.

findings.summary: Infrastructure modules inconsistently raise bare builtin exceptions (ValueError, RuntimeError) alongside the custom TemplateError hierarchy
evidence: manuscript_overview.py raises ValueError 5 times (e.g. 'PDF has no pages') inside infrastructure layer
retry.py raises RuntimeError('failed after N attempts') instead of a TemplateError subclass
menu.py raises ValueError('Empty choice'), ValueError('No valid choices found')
performance.py raises RuntimeError('Monitor not started')
The codebase has a well-defined custom exception hierarchy (18 raises of TemplateError subclasses) but these modules bypass it
findings.suggestion: Replace bare ValueError/RuntimeError raises inside infrastructure/ with appropriate TemplateError subclasses (ValidationError for invalid input, BuildError for pipeline failures).

findings.summary: Multiple except-Exception blocks only log and return empty/None without propagating, silently hiding failures from callers
evidence: llm/cli/main.py lines 85-86, 178-179: except Exception: logger.error() with no re-raise
publishing/cli.py lines 164-165, 220-221: except Exception: logger.error() with implicit None return
pipeline_reporter.py line 126-128: swallows error silently with degraded empty result
performance.py _get_memory_usage and _get_cpu_percent: except Exception: return 0.0 with no log at all — inconsistent even with the log-only pattern above
findings.suggestion: Inside library functions, either re-raise as a typed TemplateError or return a typed result indicating failure so callers can react. Bare swallows are only acceptable at top-level CLI handlers.

findings.summary: I/O boundary helpers return three different empty sentinels (None, empty-dict, False) for the same 'unreadable' condition with no consistent contract
evidence: coverage_parser.py uses four different failure-signalling conventions in one module: Optional[float], (bool, Optional[float]), (bool, str), and empty-list sentinel
integrity.py load_integrity_manifest returns None on OSError/JSONDecodeError
suite_summary_generator.py load_test_results returns {} on the same condition
Callers must check for None or empty separately with no documented contract
findings.suggestion: Establish a single convention for I/O helper return-on-failure: prefer Optional[T] with None for 'not found/unreadable'. For coverage_parser.py, align all private helpers to use (bool, value) tuples matching the public API.

findings.summary: Reporting and validation functions use Dict[str, Any] as both inputs and outputs extensively where TypedDicts already exist in the same files
evidence: generate_pipeline_report() accepts 5 Optional[Dict[str, Any]] parameters with no type safety

integrity.py functions return Dict[str, bool] and Dict[str, Any] where TypedDict variants (BuildArtifactValidation, PermissionCheck, OutputCompleteness) already exist in the same file but are unused for primary functions
Dict[str, Any] appears 203 times across 49 infrastructure files
findings.suggestion: Promote existing TypedDict classes to be used as return types for the primary verify_* functions. Extract a PipelineReportInput dataclass to replace the 5 optional dict parameters in generate_pipeline_report().

findings.summary: health_check.py exposes three module-level wrapper functions that only forward to a lazily created singleton, adding indirection with no policy
evidence: health_check.py: _get_checker() creates and caches SystemHealthChecker in a module global
quick_health_check() = _get_checker().is_healthy() — pure forward with no policy
get_health_status() = _get_checker().get_health_status() — pure forward
performance.py follows the same pattern with get_performance_monitor() -> CodeProfiler
findings.suggestion: Use a module-level instance directly without the intermediate lazy getter indirection. The pattern is repeated across at least two modules suggesting a structural habit rather than deliberate design.

findings.summary: psutil is in the optional 'monitoring' group but guarded by duplicate try/except in two separate modules with identical fallback logic
evidence: Both health_check.py and performance.py independently do 'try: import psutil except ImportError: psutil = None' with identical fallback
pyproject.toml places psutil in optional 'monitoring' group but includes it in default-groups — always installed in normal use
The dual-guard means neither module can share a single authoritative 'psutil available' check
findings.suggestion: Consolidate the psutil availability check into infrastructure/core/_optional_deps.py and import the result from there. Both modules share one authoritative availability flag.

findings.summary: requests is a hard runtime dependency but its non-LLM use (health_check network probe) could use stdlib urllib, making it unnecessary for non-LLM consumers
evidence: pyproject.toml lists requests>=2.31.0 as a hard runtime dependency
The entire llm/ subsystem is optional (requires Ollama), yet requests is not in the optional 'llm' group
health_check._check_network uses requests for HTTP probing — a lightweight use that could fall back to urllib.request
findings.suggestion: Move requests to the optional 'llm' dependency group and replace health_check network probe with urllib.request (stdlib), avoiding a non-stdlib dependency for users who never use LLM integration.

findings.summary: scripts/01_run_tests.py (1520 LOC) and optimization_analysis.py (1662 LOC) are zero-importer critical modules with no test coverage
evidence: existing_findings: 'Untested critical module (1520 LOC, 0 importers)' for scripts/01_run_tests.py
existing_findings: 'Untested critical module (1662 LOC, 0 importers)' for optimization_analysis.py
Both are large, logic-heavy scripts with no test coverage despite the 90% coverage requirement
findings.suggestion: Extract core logic from scripts/01_run_tests.py into infrastructure/reporting/ following the thin orchestrator pattern. Move analysis logic in optimization_analysis.py into projects/code_project/src/ and test it there.

findings.summary: tests/infra_tests/llm/test_logging.py contains MagicMock usage despite the project-wide absolute no-mocks requirement
evidence: Grep for MagicMock/mock.patch/unittest.mock found exactly one file: tests/infra_tests/llm/test_logging.py
CLAUDE.md states: 'ABSOLUTE REQUIREMENT: Never use MagicMock, mocker.patch, unittest.mock, or any mocking framework'
All other test files successfully avoid mocks using real HTTP servers and real data
findings.suggestion: Replace mock usage in test_logging.py with the real pytest-httpserver pattern already used in the LLM test suite (see conftest.py ollama_test_server fixture).

findings.summary: tests/infra_tests/reporting/ has no __init__.py while all 9 peer test directories do
evidence: Glob for tests/infra_tests/**/__init__.py returns results for core/, publishing/, documentation/, llm/, project/, rendering/, validation/, scientific/, steganography/ — but NOT reporting/
tests/infra_tests/reporting/ contains 11 test files (3746 LOC) with no __init__.py
findings.suggestion: Add an empty tests/infra_tests/reporting/__init__.py to match all sibling test directories and prevent import resolution edge cases during pytest collection.

findings.summary: projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/src/visualization/styles.py is imported by 3 production files but has no test coverage
evidence: blind packet testing.critical_untested: styles.py listed with importers=3
No test file targeting styles.py found in the project test suite
findings.suggestion: Add projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/tests/test_styles.py covering style object instantiation and property validation.

findings.summary: Every pass-only exception subclass in exceptions.py carries a multi-line docstring with Examples that merely restate the class name
evidence: ConfigurationError body is 'pass', preceded by a 9-line docstring listing what kinds of errors it covers and a full '>>> raise' example
MissingConfigurationError, InvalidConfigurationError, ValidationError all follow the same pattern — pass-only body with multi-line docstring
The docstring content ('This includes errors in: ...') adds no information beyond what the class name communicates
findings.suggestion: Collapse pass-only exception subclasses to a single-line docstring that states only what distinguishes this subclass from its parent. Remove Examples sections from trivial subclasses.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/__init__.py re-exports 90+ symbols spanning 18 sub-modules with no selection rationale, obscuring internal structure
evidence: __all__ contains 90+ entries spanning exceptions, logging, progress, retry, checkpoint, performance, health, security, config, environment, file ops, pipeline, multi-project
Implementation-detail types (ProfilingMetrics, StreamingProgress) are promoted to the same flat namespace as primary API entry points
The re-export surface spans 18 distinct sub-modules with no grouping signal
findings.suggestion: Restrict __init__.py re-exports to the 10-15 symbols most commonly needed by cross-module callers. Move specialist symbols to direct sub-module imports at their call sites.

findings.summary: Simple dataclass accessor methods carry multi-line docstrings that restate the method signature
evidence: performance.py: ResourceUsage.to_dict() is 6 lines mapping dataclass fields to a dict, yet carries a 'Convert to dictionary.' docstring header
health_check.py: SystemHealthChecker.get_health_status() has a full Args and Returns docstring that repeats what the type signature already states
infrastructure/core/ has dense docstrings on every method regardless of complexity
findings.suggestion: Reserve docstrings for non-obvious behaviour, side effects, or domain rules. Remove or shrink docstrings on methods whose purpose is completely expressed by their name and type signature.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ has 26 files (8684 LOC) mixing pipeline orchestration, logging subsystem (5 files), health monitoring, security, credentials, and menus — far beyond a coherent single-concern package
evidence: directory_profiles shows infrastructure/core/ at 26 files, 8684 LOC, avg_fan_in 9.7 — highest fan-in of any package

5 logging_*.py files form an implicit sub-package not surfaced as one; logging_constants.py docstring explicitly notes it exists to avoid circular imports within core/ — a signal the group has outgrown the directory
Unrelated concerns — menu.py, security.py, credentials.py — all live alongside pipeline.py with no sub-grouping
findings.suggestion: Extract the logging quartet into infrastructure/core/logging/ as a sub-package. Consider moving menu.py to infrastructure/cli/ and security.py/credentials.py to infrastructure/security/.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ previously contained test_reporter.py and test_summary_generator.py — production modules with test_ prefixes causing zone misclassification
evidence: directory_profiles for infrastructure/reporting/ showed zones: {'production': 12, 'test': 2} — scanner classified the test_-prefixed files as test zone files inside a production directory
Both were production report generators imported by scripts/, not test utilities
findings.suggestion: Renaming to coverage_reporter.py and suite_summary_generator.py (already done) resolves this. Verify all import references are updated.

findings.summary: A second HealthChecker class lives inside sanitization.py rather than health_check.py, splitting system health ownership across two unrelated modules
evidence: infrastructure/core/health_check.py defines SystemHealthChecker (canonical health monitoring)

infrastructure/llm/core/sanitization.py lines 197-305 define a second HealthChecker with overlapping responsibilities (filesystem, dependencies, network, memory checks) inside a file whose stated purpose is 'Input sanitization'
Both expose run_all_checks / get_health_status over identical concern areas; global _health_checker singleton is created at import time in sanitization.py
findings.suggestion: Remove HealthChecker from sanitization.py and consolidate into infrastructure/core/health_check.py. sanitization.py should only contain InputSanitizer and SecurityError.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ has 14 files with overlapping responsibilities and no clear ownership boundary between reporter types
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py defines generate_test_report(), generate_validation_report(), generate_performance_report() — functions logically belonging in dedicated sibling files
test_reporter.py also defines generate_test_report(), creating a duplicate public API surface
14 files at 8461 LOC for a reporting layer with no clear ownership boundary between executive, pipeline, multi-project, and summary reporters
findings.suggestion: Each reporter file should own one report type end-to-end. Remove redundant generate_test_report/generate_validation_report/etc. from pipeline_reporter.py and delegate to dedicated sibling reporters.

findings.summary: Two parallel monitoring subsystems (PerformanceMonitor and CodeProfiler) coexist in performance.py with overlapping responsibilities and inconsistent global accessor patterns
evidence: PerformanceMonitor (line 76) tracks resource usage via psutil with start/stop lifecycle
CodeProfiler (line 368) tracks timing and memory via cProfile/tracemalloc with its own history list
get_performance_monitor() returns a global CodeProfiler singleton, but benchmark_function() at module level creates a throwaway CodeProfiler() rather than using the global
findings.suggestion: Merge into one class or promote to separate modules (stage_monitor.py, function_profiler.py) with non-overlapping responsibilities. Eliminate the throwaway instantiation inconsistency.

findings.summary: SystemHealthChecker._check_filesystem() constructs paths relative to cwd at call time, making the module's contract fragile and undocumented
evidence: health_check.py:98-104: Path('projects') and Path('infrastructure') constructed without anchoring to any configured root
Correctness depends on the process working directory at the time of the call
findings.suggestion: Accept a repo_root parameter in SystemHealthChecker.__init__ and thread it through _check_filesystem, matching the pattern used by PipelineExecutor and CheckpointManager.

findings.summary: StageResult dataclass uses None sentinel fields with __post_init__ fixup instead of field(default_factory=list)
evidence: Lines 26-28: output_files, errors, warnings declared as None with type: ignore[assignment] comments
Lines 30-36: __post_init__ checks each for None and replaces with [] — standard mutable-default anti-pattern workaround
The type: ignore comments acknowledge the workaround but leave the root cause unfixed
findings.suggestion: Replace None sentinels with field(default_factory=list) for output_files, errors, warnings. Eliminates __post_init__ boilerplate and the type suppressions.

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report() accepts 9 parameters where 6 are optional Dict[str, Any] — should accept a structured context object at this boundary
evidence: Lines 273-283: stage_results, total_duration, repo_root, test_results, validation_results, performance_metrics, error_summary, output_statistics, project_name
Six of nine parameters are Optional[Dict[str, Any]] — structurally identical at the call site, distinguishable only by keyword name
findings.suggestion: Introduce a PipelineContext dataclass grouping the optional dict parameters. Callers pass one well-typed object instead of 6 keyword dicts.

findings.summary: generate_test_report() is defined in both pipeline_reporter.py and test_reporter.py, creating an ambiguous seam for callers
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py line ~663: def generate_test_report(test_results: Dict[str, Any], output_dir: Path) -> Path
coverage_reporter.py (formerly test_reporter.py) also defines generate_test_report()
Callers in the same package have no basis to choose between the two implementations
findings.suggestion: Remove generate_test_report from pipeline_reporter.py; pipeline_reporter should call coverage_reporter.generate_test_report() if needed.

findings.summary: performance_context() context manager calls monitor.stop() in finally block but discards the returned PerformanceMetrics — callers cannot access them
evidence: Lines 151-164: yield monitor; finally: metrics = monitor.stop(); logger.debug(...) — metrics is a local variable that goes out of scope immediately
Docstring says 'callers can read PerformanceMetrics after exit' but this is incorrect — monitor.stop() clears start_time and returns the metrics only to the finally block
findings.suggestion: Store metrics on the monitor instance (monitor.last_metrics = metrics) so callers can access them after the with-block, or restructure as a two-phase API.

findings.summary: get_performance_warnings() iterates self.stages three times in separate loops for slow/high-memory/high-cpu where a single pass would suffice
evidence: Lines 269-304: three separate 'for stage in self.stages' loops, each checking one metric and appending to the same warnings list
All three loops are O(n) with no interdependence — a single pass checks all three conditions per stage
findings.suggestion: Merge into a single loop body that checks all three conditions per stage.

findings.summary: generate_test_report() re-imports json at line ~676 inside the function body despite json already being imported at module level
evidence: Line 9: import json (module-level)
Line ~676: import json inside generate_test_report body — redundant and inconsistent with all other infrastructure modules
findings.suggestion: Remove the inline import; the module-level import is sufficient.

findings.summary: sanitization.py conflates LLM input sanitization with general system health monitoring, violating single responsibility
evidence: Module docstring: 'Input sanitization and security utilities for LLM operations' — no mention of health checks
InputSanitizer (lines 20-188) is tightly LLM-specific; HealthChecker (lines 197-305) checks filesystem, deps, network, memory — entirely orthogonal
Global _health_checker singleton created at module import time (line 310), triggering dependency checking on every LLM sanitization import
findings.suggestion: Move HealthChecker and helpers to infrastructure/core/health_check.py. Keep sanitization.py focused on InputSanitizer and SecurityError.

findings.summary: performance.py provides two overlapping monitoring hierarchies with near-duplicate dataclasses and inconsistent global accessor patterns
evidence: PerformanceMonitor/StagePerformanceTracker (stage-level, psutil) and CodeProfiler/ProfilingMetrics (function-level, tracemalloc/cProfile) — three distinct classes solving overlapping problems
benchmark_function() at module level creates a throwaway CodeProfiler() — duplicating what CodeProfiler.benchmark_function already does
benchmark_llm_query() leaks an LLM domain concern into a generic infrastructure module
profile_memory_usage() re-implements tracemalloc logic already inside CodeProfiler.monitor()
findings.suggestion: Separate into two focused modules (stage_monitor.py, function_profiler.py) or consolidate. Remove benchmark_llm_query from this module — it belongs in the llm subsystem.

findings.summary: The is_infra boolean parameter threads through multiple private functions in coverage_parser.py purely to toggle debug logging
evidence: _wait_for_coverage_file, _parse_coverage_json, extract_coverage_percentage all accept is_infra=False — the only functional difference is whether logger.debug() is called
The computation is identical in both branches; the parameter misuses a behavioral flag for something that belongs to logger configuration
findings.suggestion: Remove is_infra from all private function signatures. Use the module logger at DEBUG level unconditionally; control verbosity through logger configuration, not function parameters.

findings.summary: verify_output_completeness() hardcodes expected subdirectory names rather than accepting them as parameters, unlike the parameterized pattern used by sibling functions
evidence: Lines 572-625: pdf_dir = output_dir / 'pdf'; figures_dir = output_dir / 'figures'; data_dir = output_dir / 'data'; tex_dir = output_dir / 'tex' — all hardcoded
validate_build_artifacts() at line 480 correctly accepts expected_files as a parameter — the preferred pattern exists but was not applied here
findings.suggestion: Parameterize subdirectory names (pdf_subdir, figures_subdir, etc.) or accept an expected layout config object, matching the pattern used by validate_build_artifacts().
